# The Interoperability Boundary: Where Governed Autonomy Meets the Agentic Web

The architectural principles governing multi-agent AI systems have matured along two largely independent trajectories. One trajectory addresses the internal coordination problem: how agents within a shared trust boundary decompose tasks, delegate work, manage memory, retrieve knowledge, and maintain auditable execution traces. The other trajectory addresses the external coordination problem: how heterogeneous agents across organizational, technical, and trust boundaries discover one another, negotiate collaboration, and form dynamic coalitions. The existing literature on governed scientific multi-agent systems and adversarial resilience has thoroughly characterized the internal trajectory, establishing that governance and security are co-designable through explicit persistence boundaries, provenance-grounded trust, compartmentalized retrieval, and contract-bounded autonomy. The vision of an Agentic Web has articulated the external trajectory's aspirations—seamless cross-platform discovery, recursive delegation across organizational lines, and swarm-based self-organization. What neither trajectory has adequately addressed is the architectural interface between them: the mechanisms required when a governed agent system operating under strict internal trust policies must interact with external agents whose governance posture, memory integrity, and provenance infrastructure are unknown, incompatible, or absent.

This interface problem is not merely a protocol design challenge analogous to standardizing message formats. It is a fundamental extension of the governance–trust co-design problem into a regime where the co-design assumptions break down. Within a single governed deployment, the trust architecture rests on the premise that persistence boundaries, retrieval lifecycle policies, delegation depth limits, and provenance chains are all controlled by a unified configuration authority. The system administrator or scientist who specifies which agents are available, which tools they may invoke, and which corpora they may access simultaneously defines the governance posture and the security perimeter. When an agent operating under such governance must delegate a subtask to an external agent discovered through an open protocol—one built on a different framework, operating under different memory policies, and potentially lacking provenance infrastructure entirely—every assumption underlying the internal trust chain is suspended at the boundary crossing.

The driver–helper hierarchy illustrates this concretely. Within a governed system, the separation between reasoning (drivers) and execution (helpers) enforces a clean boundary that prevents uncontrolled side effects and enables compartmentalized trust. A helper agent queries only its assigned corpora, returns structured results to its coordinating driver, and operates under explicit capability constraints. But when the "helper" is an external agent discovered through a cross-organizational protocol, the governed system has no mechanism to verify that the external agent respects analogous constraints. The external agent's memory may include automatically injected historical context from prior interactions with other systems—precisely the kind of implicit state that governed architectures reject for both reproducibility and security reasons. Its retrieval indices may include content that would be designated non-indexable under the governed system's policies. Its execution traces may be incomplete, unverifiable, or absent entirely.

The contract-bounded autonomy model—where agents operate freely within boundaries defined at initialization but cannot silently expand beyond them—provides the conceptual foundation for addressing this, but requires extension into a regime of negotiated rather than configured boundaries. Within a single deployment, contracts are specified by a human authority and enforced by the runtime. Across organizational boundaries, contracts must be established dynamically between agents whose respective authorities may have incompatible governance requirements. An agent operating under strict provenance requirements for scientific reproducibility cannot simply accept execution results from an external agent that lacks provenance infrastructure; but refusing all external collaboration defeats the purpose of interoperability. The missing architectural element is a trust negotiation layer that can establish, at the boundary between governed and external systems, what provenance guarantees the external agent can provide, what verification mechanisms are available, and what trust level the governed system should assign to results that cross the boundary.

This trust negotiation is qualitatively different from the internal trust mechanisms that existing architectures provide. Internally, provenance-grounded trust works because the system controls the execution environment and can cryptographically verify that recorded outcomes match actual execution states. Externally, the governed system has no access to the foreign agent's execution environment and cannot independently verify its claims about provenance. The self-referential trust failure identified in adversarial analysis—where the same reasoning capabilities used to generate content are used to evaluate its trustworthiness—reappears at the inter-system level in a new form: the governed system must use its own reasoning to evaluate whether an external agent's claimed governance posture is genuine, and a sophisticated adversarial agent could present fabricated provenance metadata that satisfies surface-level verification.

The resolution likely requires a layered trust architecture at the interoperability boundary. Results from external agents would enter the governed system not into the primary memory compartments used for internal execution traces but into a quarantined retrieval pool with explicitly lower trust status—analogous to the compartmentalized retrieval pools that separate system-generated records from user-influenced content, but extended to separate internally governed records from externally sourced ones. Budgeted context management would limit the influence of externally sourced results on any single reasoning step. The governed system's execution traces would record the boundary crossing explicitly, including the external agent's identity, claimed capabilities, and the trust level assigned to its contributions, preserving the auditability chain even when provenance cannot be independently verified beyond the boundary.

The cold start problem acquires an additional dimension in this context. When a governed system first encounters a class of external agents—through a new discovery protocol or a newly available collaboration partner—it has no execution history with which to calibrate trust. The internal cold start mitigation strategy of seeding memory banks with verified traces from controlled environments has no direct analogue for external agents, whose controlled environments are inaccessible. Initial interactions with external agents would need to be sandboxed more aggressively than steady-state interactions, with results treated as provisional until a sufficient history of verified outcomes accumulates—a form of trust bootstrapping that parallels but extends the Thompson sampling exploration phase in model routing, applied now not to model selection but to partner selection across organizational boundaries.

The Model Context Protocol addresses part of this problem by standardizing context sharing, but it operates within a shared trust boundary and does not provide mechanisms for the trust negotiation, provenance verification, or compartmentalized integration that cross-boundary collaboration requires. The Agentic Web vision of seamless discovery and dynamic coalition formation implicitly assumes that interoperability is primarily a protocol problem—that if agents can communicate, they can collaborate. The governance and security analysis reveals that communication is necessary but insufficient: agents can collaborate safely only when the trust properties of their interaction are explicitly negotiated, architecturally enforced, and traceably recorded. The gap between internal governance and external interoperability is where the next generation of multi-agent architecture must be designed, and the principles developed for governed scientific systems—explicit boundaries, provenance-grounded trust, compartmentalized access, budgeted influence, and lifecycle-managed state—provide the vocabulary, if not yet the complete grammar, for that design.

## See Also

- [[z20260318-012|The Governance–Trust Co-Design Problem]] — grounds the convergence of governance and security requirements within a single trust boundary that this note extends to cross-boundary interactions
- [[z20260318-005|Trust as the Unifying Vulnerability Across Memory, Routing, and Multi-Agent Coordination]] — grounds the self-referential trust failure and exogenous trust anchor requirements that reappear in amplified form at interoperability boundaries
- [[z20260318-011|Persistent Memory as a Governed Resource]] — grounds the compartmentalized retrieval, provenance-grounded trust, and lifecycle-managed indexing principles that must be extended to handle externally sourced memory
- [[z20260318-010|Multi-Agent Systems for AI-Assisted Scientific Reasoning]] — grounds the contract-bounded autonomy, driver–helper hierarchy, and retrieval governance mechanisms whose assumptions are suspended at cross-organizational boundaries
- [[z20260318-001|Multi-Agent Architectures and Procedural Memory for Workflow Automation]] — contrasts the experience-reuse orientation that motivates cross-boundary collaboration with the trust constraints that govern it
